---
title: "The Estimator as a Random Variable"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Statistical Inference"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

POINT  = "#2b3a67"     # data / samples
LINE   = "#c1121f"     # the curve / theoretical / true value
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"     # highlights / the estimate
GREY   = "#6c757d"
FILL   = "#9fb3d1"     # shaded areas / histograms
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the entry point of the **Statistical
> Inference** track — the first article, assuming nothing from inside the track. From the
> Probability Foundations track it leans on three things, each built there from the ground
> up: **random variables, expectation, and variance** ([PROB-02], [PROB-05], [PROB-06]);
> **independence and the i.i.d. sample** ([PROB-08]); and the fact, derived in [PROB-08],
> that the variance of a sample mean is $\sigma^2/n$. Everything else is on this page.
> Where Probability asks *"given a known distribution, what does a sample look like?"*,
> Inference reverses the arrow: *"given a sample, what can we say about the unknown
> distribution that produced it?"* The hinge that makes the reversal possible is the
> single idea this article installs — that **the number an estimator computes is itself a
> random variable**, with a distribution we can describe, simulate, and reason about. It
> sets up [INFR-A2] (the two limit theorems that pin down the *shape* of that
> distribution), [INFR-B1] (the likelihood, our first principled way to build an
> estimator), and [INFR-C1] (confidence intervals, which exist only because an estimator
> has a sampling distribution to begin with).

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

An **estimator** is a function of the sample — a recipe like "average the numbers" — and
because the sample is random, the number that recipe returns is *itself a random
variable*. This single shift in viewpoint is the foundation of the entire track. This
article builds the **sampling distribution** of an estimator from first principles: the
distribution of its output across all the samples we *might* have drawn. We derive, every
step shown, that the sample mean $\bar X_n$ has expectation equal to the true mean $\mu$
and standard deviation $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$ — the quantity we name the **standard error** —
and we confirm the same answer by exact algebra and by simulation. We define **bias** and
**variance** as the two ways an estimator can miss, and prove the **mean-squared-error
decomposition** $\mathrm{MSE} = \text{bias}^2 + \text{variance}$ in full. The single claim
the article defends is that **every estimate you will ever report is one draw from a
distribution you never see** — and that quantifying that distribution, not the single
number, is what inference *is*.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
An **estimator** $\hat\theta = g(X_1,\dots,X_n)$ is a function of the random sample, so it
is **itself a random variable**: re-run the experiment, draw a fresh sample, and the
estimate changes. The distribution of those would-be values is the **sampling
distribution**. Two numbers summarize how well the estimator does its job. Its **bias**,
$\mathbb{E}[\hat\theta]-\theta$, is how far off it sits *on average*; its **standard
error**, $\mathrm{SE}=\sqrt{\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta)}$, is how much it bounces from
sample to sample. Total error combines both, and combines them in exactly one way:
$$
\mathrm{MSE}(\hat\theta) = \mathbb{E}\!\bigl[(\hat\theta-\theta)^2\bigr]
= \underbrace{\bigl(\mathbb{E}[\hat\theta]-\theta\bigr)^2}_{\text{bias}^2}
\;+\; \underbrace{\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta)}_{\text{variance}}.
$$
The estimate you report is one draw from this distribution. **Inference is the discipline
of describing the distribution, not the draw.**
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The most important sentence is the quiet one: *the
estimate is one draw*. A coder's instinct is to treat the output of a function as a fixed
answer — you ran `mean(data)` and got `4.7`, end of story. The whole of this track refuses
that instinct. Feed the same recipe a different sample from the same world and you get
`4.4`, or `5.1`. Those alternative answers are not errors; they are the *other realizations
of the same random variable*, and their spread — captured by the standard error — is the
honest measure of how seriously to take the `4.7` you happened to see.

# Why we are doing this: the problem that forced the idea {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the entire article: **why is a computed estimate a random
variable, and what does treating it as one buy us?** Every definition and derivation below
is an answer to some piece of that question. If a step feels abstract, return here.

## The original problem: one sample, infinite worlds it could have been

The reason this track exists is a gap that opens the instant you collect data. You measure
the cholesterol of $200$ patients and compute the average, $\bar x = 193$ mg/dL. You want
to say something about *the population* — all patients like these, not just the $200$ you
happened to enroll. But you did not measure the population. You measured a **sample**, and
a different random draw of $200$ patients would have handed you a different average:
$191$, or $196$. Which one is "the" answer?

The conceptual leap — made gradually through the nineteenth century, from Gauss and
Laplace formalizing the "error" in astronomical measurements, through Quetelet applying
the same idea to human populations, to Karl Pearson and R. A. Fisher in the early
twentieth century turning it into a discipline — was to stop asking *"which sample average
is right?"* and start asking *"what is the distribution of the sample average across all
the samples I might have drawn?"* That distribution is the **sampling distribution**, and
once you have it, the original anxiety dissolves. You no longer pretend $\bar x = 193$ is
the truth. You say: my estimator centers on the true mean and scatters around it with a
spread I can compute, and $193$ is one realization from that scatter. *That* is a
defensible statement, and producing defensible statements is the charter of this track.

Fisher gave the central object its modern name in his 1922 paper *On the Mathematical
Foundations of Theoretical Statistics* — he called a sample-based recipe a **statistic**,
and insisted its **sampling distribution** was the proper subject of the theory. The word
*estimator* (the recipe) versus *estimate* (the number it returns on one sample) is the
distinction that the rest of this article makes precise and never lets go.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What is the difference between an estimator and an estimate? Interviewers love this one."*
An **estimator** is a *function* — a rule, written before you see data, that turns any
sample into a number: $\hat\theta = g(X_1,\dots,X_n)$, with capital $X_i$ to signal the
sample is still random. An **estimate** is the *value* that rule returns on the one sample
you actually collected: $\hat\theta = g(x_1,\dots,x_n)$, lowercase, a fixed number like
$193$. The estimator is a random variable and has a distribution; the estimate is one
realization of it. Sloppy speech blurs them, but the entire track hinges on keeping them
apart: you cannot ask for "the variance of $193$" — a constant has no variance — but you
can and must ask for the variance of the estimator that produced it.
:::

## What came before, and why it was not enough

Before this viewpoint, a measurement was just a number, and the only acknowledgement of
uncertainty was the vague hedge "give or take a bit." That is useless for a decision: how
much is "a bit"? Is a drug that lowers cholesterol by $4$ mg/dL on a sample of $30$ a real
effect or a fluke of who walked into the clinic? You cannot answer without a *model of how
much the estimate would wobble under resampling*. The sampling-distribution viewpoint
turns "give or take a bit" into a specific number — the standard error — and turns "real
effect or fluke?" into a calculation. Everything downstream in the track (confidence
intervals, hypothesis tests, the bootstrap) is machinery for getting at that distribution
when the simple algebra of this article runs out.

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Picture a factory. The **population** is an enormous, fixed urn of numbers — every
patient's cholesterol, say — with a true but unknown mean $\mu$ and standard deviation
$\sigma$. You cannot see the urn. You are allowed one action: reach in and draw $n$
numbers at random, the **sample**. You feed those $n$ numbers into a recipe (the
estimator) and out comes a single number (the estimate).

Now imagine doing this not once but *thousands* of times — draw $n$, compute the estimate,
write it down, return the numbers to the urn, repeat. You accumulate a long list of
estimates, all targeting the same fixed $\mu$, all slightly different because each came
from a different lucky-or-unlucky handful. **Histogram that list.** The shape you get is
the sampling distribution. It has a center (where the estimates pile up) and a spread (how
widely they scatter). The center tells you whether the recipe aims at the truth; the
spread tells you how much to trust any single estimate. @fig-factory draws this factory
explicitly: many samples, many estimates, one histogram.

```{python}
#| label: fig-factory
#| fig-cap: 'The estimator factory. A fixed population (left, true mean $\mu$ marked in red) is sampled many times; each sample of size $n=30$ is fed to the recipe ''take the mean'' and yields one estimate. Collecting thousands of those estimates and histogramming them (right) reveals the *sampling distribution* of $\bar X_n$: it centers on the true $\mu$ and is far narrower than the population itself. The single estimate you would actually compute is just one of the bars in that histogram.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

# A skewed, decidedly non-normal population so the picture is honest.
pop = rng.gamma(shape=2.0, scale=2.0, size=200_000)
mu_true = pop.mean()
sigma_true = pop.std()

n = 30
n_reps = 4000
means = np.array([rng.choice(pop, size=n, replace=False).mean() for _ in range(n_reps)])

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.7))

axL.hist(pop, bins=80, color=FILL, edgecolor="none")
axL.axvline(mu_true, color=LINE, lw=2.0, label=fr"true mean $\mu={mu_true:.2f}$")
axL.set_title("The population (one fixed urn)")
axL.set_xlabel("value"); axL.set_ylabel("count")
axL.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
axL.set_xlim(0, 20)

axR.hist(means, bins=40, color=FILL, edgecolor="white", density=True)
axR.axvline(mu_true, color=LINE, lw=2.0, label=fr"true mean $\mu={mu_true:.2f}$")
axR.axvline(means.mean(), color=ACCENT, lw=2.0, ls="--",
            label=fr"mean of estimates $={means.mean():.2f}$")
axR.set_title(fr"Sampling distribution of $\bar X_n$  ($n={n}$)")
axR.set_xlabel(r"value of the estimate $\bar x$"); axR.set_ylabel("density")
axR.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()

print(f"population mean   mu      = {mu_true:.3f}")
print(f"population stddev sigma   = {sigma_true:.3f}")
print(f"mean of the 4000 estimates= {means.mean():.3f}   (lands on mu)")
print(f"stddev of the estimates   = {means.std():.3f}")
print(f"sigma / sqrt(n)           = {sigma_true/np.sqrt(n):.3f}   (matches the spread)")
```

Two features of @fig-factory are the whole article in miniature, and we will *derive* both
rather than leave them as pictures. First, the histogram of estimates is **centered on the
true $\mu$** — the recipe aims true. Second, it is **dramatically narrower than the
population** — averaging $30$ noisy numbers gives a far steadier output than any single
number, and the printed line shows the spread is almost exactly $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$. The
narrowing is *why we collect more than one observation*, and the rate of narrowing
($1/\sqrt{n}$) is one of the most consequential numbers in all of statistics.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"The population in that figure is skewed and lumpy — yet the sampling distribution looks
almost like a bell curve. Why?"* That is the **Central Limit Theorem** showing its hand,
and it is the subject of the very next article, [INFR-A2]. The headline: averages of i.i.d.
quantities tend toward a normal shape *regardless* of the population's shape, as $n$ grows.
This article deliberately stops *before* claiming the shape — we will pin down the
sampling distribution's **center and spread** exactly and for any $n$, because those need
no limit theorem. The *shape* is genuinely harder and earns its own article. Knowing which
facts are exact-and-cheap (center, spread) versus deep-and-asymptotic (the bell shape) is
itself an interview-grade distinction.
:::

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

## The two players: population parameter and sample statistic {#sec-two-players}

We must be ruthless about a distinction that informal language smears together. There is a
**population** — the entire collection of units we care about, or equivalently the
probability distribution that generated our data. It has fixed, true, but *unknown*
numerical features. There is a **sample** — the finite batch of data we actually observe,
drawn at random from that population.

::: {#def-parameter-statistic}
**Definition (parameter vs. statistic).** A **parameter** is a fixed numerical feature of
the population (or its generating distribution) — for instance the true mean $\mu$, the
true variance $\sigma^2$, or the true success probability $p$. It is an unknown *constant*:
it does not vary, we just do not know it. A **statistic** is any quantity computed
entirely from the observed sample — for instance the sample mean $\bar x$ or the sample
proportion $\hat p$. Because the sample is random, a statistic is a *random variable*.
:::

The word **estimator** is reserved for a statistic that we have nominated to *stand in for*
a particular parameter. Writing $\theta$ (Greek, the convention for the unknown) for the
parameter and $\hat\theta$ (theta-with-a-hat, the convention for "our guess at $\theta$")
for the estimator:

::: {#def-estimator}
**Definition (estimator).** Let $X_1,\dots,X_n$ be a random sample (the $X_i$ are random
variables). An **estimator** of a parameter $\theta$ is a function of the sample,
$$
\hat\theta \;=\; g(X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n),
$$ {#eq-estimator}
that contains *no unknown quantities* — you can compute it from data alone. Because it is a
function of random variables, $\hat\theta$ is itself a random variable.
:::

The clause "*no unknown quantities*" is not pedantry; it is what makes an estimator
*usable*. "$\bar X_n - \mu$" is a function of the sample and $\mu$, but you cannot compute
it without knowing $\mu$ — the very thing you are trying to estimate — so it is not an
estimator. "$\frac1n\sum X_i$" uses only data, so it is. Two concrete estimators carry the
whole article:

- The **sample mean** $\displaystyle \bar X_n = \frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n X_i$, our estimator of
  the population mean $\mu$.
- The **sample proportion** $\displaystyle \hat p = \frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n X_i$ where each
  $X_i\in\{0,1\}$ is a yes/no outcome, our estimator of the population success probability
  $p$. (It is literally a sample mean of $0$s and $1$s — a fact we will exploit.)

::: {.callout-important title="The pivot: the recipe is fixed, the input is random"}
In ordinary programming, a function applied to fixed inputs gives a fixed output, and you
stop thinking about it. Here the *function* $g$ is fixed and known — it is your chosen
recipe — but its *inputs* $X_1,\dots,X_n$ are random, because they are a random draw from
the population. Randomness flows through the function: a random input makes a random
output. This is the entire reason $\hat\theta$ has a distribution. The output's randomness
is inherited, not intrinsic — and it is inherited from the sampling, which is why the
distribution is called the *sampling* distribution.
:::

## The sampling distribution, from first principles {#sec-sampling-dist}

::: {#def-sampling-dist}
**Definition (sampling distribution).** The **sampling distribution** of an estimator
$\hat\theta = g(X_1,\dots,X_n)$ is the probability distribution of the random variable
$\hat\theta$, induced by the randomness of the sample. Concretely: it is the distribution
of values $g(x_1,\dots,x_n)$ obtained as $(x_1,\dots,x_n)$ ranges over all samples of size
$n$ that the population could produce, each weighted by how probable that sample is.
:::

Read that definition operationally and it *is* the factory of @fig-factory: enumerate (or
in practice, simulate) all possible samples, run each through $g$, and the spread of
outputs — weighted by probability — is the sampling distribution. Nothing mysterious is
happening; it is the ordinary "distribution of a function of random variables" from
probability theory, named specially because the function is an estimator and the random
variables are a sample.

Two summaries of this distribution do almost all the work in practice, and both have names.

::: {#def-se}
**Definition (sampling expectation, bias, and standard error).** For an estimator
$\hat\theta$ of a parameter $\theta$:

- its **sampling mean** is $\mathbb{E}[\hat\theta]$ — the center of the sampling
  distribution, the long-run average of the estimate over infinitely many resamples;
- its **bias** is $\operatorname{Bias}(\hat\theta) = \mathbb{E}[\hat\theta] - \theta$ —
  how far that center sits from the truth; an estimator with bias $0$ is called
  **unbiased**;
- its **standard error** is
  $\operatorname{SE}(\hat\theta) = \sqrt{\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta)}$ — the standard
  deviation of the sampling distribution, i.e. the typical sample-to-sample wobble of the
  estimate.
:::

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**The standard error is not the standard deviation of your data.** The sample standard
deviation $s$ measures how spread out the individual observations are; the standard error
measures how spread out the *estimate* is across hypothetical resamples. They are
different quantities with different units of meaning, and confusing them is one of the most
common errors in applied work. For the sample mean they are related — $\operatorname{SE} =
\sigma/\sqrt{n}$, as we derive next — but that relationship has the crucial $\sqrt{n}$ in
it precisely *because* one describes data and the other describes an average of data. When
someone reports "$193 \pm 2$," the $2$ is a standard error (how sure we are of the mean),
not the spread of patients (which might be $\pm 40$).
:::

## The sampling distribution of the sample mean, derived exactly {#sec-mean-exact}

We now compute the center and spread of $\bar X_n$ exactly — no simulation, no limit
theorem, just the algebra of expectation and variance, every step shown. The only
ingredients are the i.i.d. assumption and two rules established in the Probability track,
which we state before using.

**Imported facts (and where they are built).** We use, but do not re-derive here, two
properties proved in [PROB-05], [PROB-06], and [PROB-08]:

1. **Linearity of expectation:** for any random variables $Y_i$ and constants $a_i$,
   $\mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_i a_i Y_i\right] = \sum_i a_i \mathbb{E}[Y_i]$. This holds
   *always* — no independence required.
2. **Variance of a sum of independent variables, and scaling:**
   $\operatorname{Var}(aY) = a^2\operatorname{Var}(Y)$ for a constant $a$, and if the $Y_i$
   are independent then
   $\operatorname{Var}\!\left(\sum_i Y_i\right) = \sum_i \operatorname{Var}(Y_i)$.

We assume the sample is **i.i.d.**: the $X_i$ are independent and each has the same
distribution as the population, so each has $\mathbb{E}[X_i] = \mu$ and
$\operatorname{Var}(X_i) = \sigma^2$.

::: {#thm-mean-sampling}
**Theorem (mean and variance of the sample mean).** If $X_1,\dots,X_n$ are i.i.d. with
mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$, then the sample mean $\bar X_n = \frac1n\sum_i X_i$
satisfies
$$
\mathbb{E}[\bar X_n] = \mu,
\qquad
\operatorname{Var}(\bar X_n) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n},
\qquad
\operatorname{SE}(\bar X_n) = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}.
$$
In words: $\bar X_n$ is an **unbiased** estimator of $\mu$, and its standard error shrinks
like $1/\sqrt{n}$.
:::

::: {.proof}
**The expectation.** Start from the definition and pull the constant $\frac1n$ out of the
expectation, then apply linearity (rule 1):
$$
\mathbb{E}[\bar X_n]
= \mathbb{E}\!\left[\frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\right]
= \frac1n\,\mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\right]
= \frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n \mathbb{E}[X_i].
$$
Each $\mathbb{E}[X_i] = \mu$ because the $X_i$ are identically distributed, so the sum is
$n$ copies of $\mu$:
$$
\mathbb{E}[\bar X_n] = \frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n \mu = \frac1n\,(n\mu) = \mu.
$$
The center of the sampling distribution is exactly the truth — that is what "unbiased"
means, and note it required *no* independence, only that each observation is centered on
$\mu$.

**The variance.** Start again from the definition. Pull the constant out, but now it is
*inside* a variance, so by the scaling rule $\operatorname{Var}(aY)=a^2\operatorname{Var}(Y)$
with $a = \frac1n$ it comes out **squared**:
$$
\operatorname{Var}(\bar X_n)
= \operatorname{Var}\!\left(\frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\right)
= \frac{1}{n^2}\,\operatorname{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\right).
$$
Here is where independence earns its keep. Because the $X_i$ are independent, the variance
of their sum is the sum of their variances (rule 2) — the cross terms that would otherwise
appear all vanish:
$$
\operatorname{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^n \operatorname{Var}(X_i)
= \sum_{i=1}^n \sigma^2 = n\sigma^2,
$$
again $n$ identical copies, now of $\sigma^2$. Substitute back:
$$
\operatorname{Var}(\bar X_n) = \frac{1}{n^2}\,(n\sigma^2) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}.
$$
**The standard error** is the square root of the variance:
$$
\operatorname{SE}(\bar X_n) = \sqrt{\operatorname{Var}(\bar X_n)} = \sqrt{\frac{\sigma^2}{n}}
= \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}. \qquad\blacksquare
$$
:::

Three things deserve to be read off this result, because they answer questions the reader
is already forming.

- **Why the square in $n^2$, and why the survivor is $\sigma^2/n$ not $\sigma^2/n^2$.** The
  $\frac1n$ pulled out as $\frac{1}{n^2}$ (variance squares constants), but the sum of $n$
  independent variances contributed a factor of $n$ back, and $\frac{1}{n^2}\cdot n =
  \frac1n$. The two $n$'s do not cancel to nothing; they leave exactly one factor of $1/n$.
- **Why $1/\sqrt{n}$, not $1/n$.** Precision is measured by the *standard* error — the
  square root of the variance — and $\sqrt{\sigma^2/n} = \sigma/\sqrt{n}$. So to *halve*
  your standard error you must *quadruple* your sample. This is the diminishing-returns law
  of data collection, and it falls straight out of the square root.
- **The recipe matters: averaging beats not-averaging.** A *single* observation $X_1$ is
  also an unbiased estimator of $\mu$ (its expectation is $\mu$), but its standard error is
  the full $\sigma$. Averaging $n$ of them divides that by $\sqrt{n}$. Same target,
  $\sqrt{n}$ times less wobble — the entire payoff of collecting a sample instead of a
  single measurement.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"You used independence for the variance but not for the mean — what breaks if the data
are dependent?"* The **unbiasedness survives** dependence: $\mathbb{E}[\bar X_n]=\mu$ used
only linearity, which never needs independence, so a correlated sample still centers on the
truth. But the **variance formula breaks**. With dependence the cross-covariances
$\operatorname{Cov}(X_i,X_j)$ no longer vanish, and the true variance of $\bar X_n$
picks up $\frac{1}{n^2}\sum_{i\ne j}\operatorname{Cov}(X_i,X_j)$ on top of $\sigma^2/n$.
For *positively* correlated data (the usual case — think consecutive days of a time
series, or students in the same classroom) those covariances are positive, the real
standard error is *larger* than $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$, and naively using $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$ makes
you **overconfident**. This is exactly why "effective sample size" is smaller than $n$ for
clustered or autocorrelated data, and it is one of the most common ways real analyses go
wrong. We meet the honest version of this at @sec-failure.
:::

## The estimable problem: we never know $\sigma$ {#sec-plug-in}

The standard error $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$ contains $\sigma$, the population standard deviation —
which, like $\mu$, we do not know. So $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$ is not yet an estimator: it
violates the "no unknown quantities" clause of @def-estimator. In practice we plug in the
**sample** standard deviation $s$ to get the **estimated standard error** $s/\sqrt{n}$,
where
$$
s^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \bar X_n)^2.
$$ {#eq-sample-var}

Why the divisor $n-1$ and not $n$? Because dividing by $n$ gives a *biased* estimator of
$\sigma^2$ — it systematically underestimates — and $n-1$ corrects exactly that bias. The
short reason: the deviations are taken from $\bar X_n$, not the true $\mu$, and $\bar X_n$
is by construction the value that makes $\sum (X_i-\bar X_n)^2$ as small as possible, so it
under-counts the spread; the correction restores it. We prove this carefully, because it is
a clean and famous worked example of *bias*, in @sec-example-variance below. For now,
register that the *estimated* standard error introduces a small extra layer of uncertainty
(we estimated $\sigma$ too), and that handling that extra layer honestly is precisely what
forces the $t$-distribution in [INFR-C2]. This article uses the *exact* $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$
when illustrating, so the algebra stays clean.

## The sampling distribution of the sample proportion {#sec-proportion}

The second canonical estimator is the **sample proportion** $\hat p$, the natural estimator
of a true success probability $p$ — the fraction of patients who respond to a drug, of
emails that are spam, of voters favoring a candidate. We get its sampling distribution for
free, because $\hat p$ *is* a sample mean.

Code each observation as $X_i = 1$ for a "success" and $X_i = 0$ for a "failure". Then $X_i$
is a **Bernoulli$(p)$** random variable, and the sample proportion is literally their
average:
$$
\hat p = \frac{\text{number of successes}}{n} = \frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n X_i = \bar X_n.
$$
So @thm-mean-sampling applies verbatim once we know the mean and variance of a single
Bernoulli. We derive both rather than quote them.

A Bernoulli$(p)$ variable takes value $1$ with probability $p$ and $0$ with probability
$1-p$. Its expectation is the probability-weighted sum of its values:
$$
\mathbb{E}[X_i] = (1)\cdot p + (0)\cdot(1-p) = p.
$$
For the variance, use the computational identity $\operatorname{Var}(X) = \mathbb{E}[X^2]
- (\mathbb{E}[X])^2$ (derived in [PROB-06]). Because $X_i\in\{0,1\}$, we have $X_i^2 = X_i$
(since $0^2=0$ and $1^2=1$), so $\mathbb{E}[X_i^2] = \mathbb{E}[X_i] = p$. Therefore
$$
\operatorname{Var}(X_i) = \mathbb{E}[X_i^2] - (\mathbb{E}[X_i])^2 = p - p^2 = p(1-p).
$$
Now feed $\mu = p$ and $\sigma^2 = p(1-p)$ into @thm-mean-sampling:
$$
\boxed{\;
\mathbb{E}[\hat p] = p,
\qquad
\operatorname{Var}(\hat p) = \frac{p(1-p)}{n},
\qquad
\operatorname{SE}(\hat p) = \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}.
\;}
$$ {#eq-prop-se}

So $\hat p$ is unbiased for $p$, and its standard error again carries the $1/\sqrt{n}$. One
extra fact, visible in @eq-prop-se and worth carrying: the standard error depends on $p$
itself, and $p(1-p)$ is largest at $p=\tfrac12$ (a fair coin is the hardest proportion to
pin down) and shrinks toward $0$ as $p$ approaches $0$ or $1$ (a near-certain outcome is
easy to estimate). @fig-binomial-sampling shows the sampling distribution of $\hat p$ built
two ways — by the *exact* binomial probabilities and by simulation — and confirms they
agree.

```{python}
#| label: fig-binomial-sampling
#| fig-cap: 'Sampling distribution of the sample proportion $\hat p$ for $n=40$, true $p=0.3$, built two ways. Grey bars: the *exact* distribution, computed from the binomial probabilities $P(\text{count}=k)=\binom{n}{k}p^k(1-p)^{n-k}$ placed at $\hat p = k/n$ — no simulation. Blue outline: a histogram of 20,000 simulated $\hat p$ values. They coincide. The red line marks the true $p$; the estimate centers there, with spread matching $\sqrt{p(1-p)/n}$ (annotated).'
from math import comb

n = 40
p = 0.3
ks = np.arange(0, n + 1)
phat_grid = ks / n
# Exact binomial pmf, computed termwise so nothing is hidden.
exact_pmf = np.array([comb(n, int(k)) * p**k * (1 - p)**(n - k) for k in ks])

rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
sims = rng.binomial(n, p, size=20_000) / n

se_exact = np.sqrt(p * (1 - p) / n)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.0, 4.2))
ax.bar(phat_grid, exact_pmf / (1 / n), width=1 / n * 0.95, color=FILL,
       edgecolor="white", label="exact (binomial)", align="center")
ax.hist(sims, bins=np.arange(-0.5, n + 1.5) / n, density=True,
        histtype="step", color=POINT, lw=1.8, label="simulated (20,000 draws)")
ax.axvline(p, color=LINE, lw=2.0, label=fr"true $p={p}$")
ax.annotate(fr"SE $=\sqrt{{p(1-p)/n}}={se_exact:.3f}$",
            xy=(p + se_exact, 4.0), xytext=(p + 0.12, 6.5),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color=GREY), fontsize=10, color=GREY)
ax.set_xlabel(r"value of the estimate $\hat p$"); ax.set_ylabel("density")
ax.set_title(r"Exact vs. simulated sampling distribution of $\hat p$")
ax.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
ax.set_xlim(0, 0.65)
plt.tight_layout()

print(f"exact mean of phat      = {np.sum(phat_grid * exact_pmf):.4f}   (= p = {p})")
print(f"exact SE  of phat       = {np.sqrt(np.sum((phat_grid - p)**2 * exact_pmf)):.4f}")
print(f"formula   sqrt(p(1-p)/n)= {se_exact:.4f}")
print(f"simulated SE            = {sims.std():.4f}")
```

The point of building it *both* ways is methodological: the exact bars come from pure
probability (the binomial pmf, every term computed), the outline comes from brute-force
simulation, and their agreement is a sanity check that "sampling distribution" is not a
vague idea but a concrete, computable object. When a closed form exists, we can write it
down; when it does not (the situation that eventually forces the bootstrap, [INFR-E1]),
simulation is how we see the same distribution anyway.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"The count of successes is Binomial$(n,p)$ — so why call $\hat p$'s distribution something
new?"* It is the same distribution, rescaled. The success *count* $K=\sum X_i$ is
Binomial$(n,p)$, living on $\{0,1,\dots,n\}$; the *proportion* $\hat p = K/n$ is that same
random variable divided by $n$, living on $\{0, \tfrac1n, \dots, 1\}$. Dividing by the
constant $n$ rescales the axis but does not change the underlying probabilities — which is
exactly why $\mathbb{E}[\hat p] = \mathbb{E}[K]/n = np/n = p$ and $\operatorname{Var}(\hat
p) = \operatorname{Var}(K)/n^2 = np(1-p)/n^2 = p(1-p)/n$. The "sampling distribution of
$\hat p$" is just the binomial wearing the units of a proportion.
:::

# Bias, variance, and the mean-squared-error decomposition {#sec-mse}

We have two ways to judge an estimator — bias (does it aim true?) and variance (how much
does it wobble?). The natural single number that combines them is the **mean squared
error**: the average squared distance between the estimate and the truth, over all
samples.

::: {#def-mse}
**Definition (mean squared error).** The **mean squared error** of an estimator
$\hat\theta$ for a parameter $\theta$ is
$$
\mathrm{MSE}(\hat\theta) = \mathbb{E}\!\bigl[(\hat\theta - \theta)^2\bigr],
$$ {#eq-mse-def}
the expectation taken over the sampling distribution of $\hat\theta$ (i.e. over all
possible samples). It is in the squared units of $\theta$.
:::

The MSE looks like it might depend on bias and variance in some tangled way. It does not —
it splits cleanly into the two, and the split is one of the most useful identities in
estimation theory. We derive it in full.

::: {#thm-mse-decomp}
**Theorem (bias–variance decomposition).** For any estimator $\hat\theta$ of a (fixed,
non-random) parameter $\theta$,
$$
\mathrm{MSE}(\hat\theta)
= \underbrace{\bigl(\mathbb{E}[\hat\theta] - \theta\bigr)^2}_{\textstyle\operatorname{Bias}(\hat\theta)^2}
\;+\; \underbrace{\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta)}_{\text{variance}}.
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
Write $\mu_{\hat\theta} = \mathbb{E}[\hat\theta]$ for the center of the sampling
distribution — a fixed number, since it is an expectation. The trick is the oldest one in
this kind of algebra: **add and subtract $\mu_{\hat\theta}$** inside the square, turning
one gap into two whose meanings we know. Starting from @eq-mse-def,
$$
\mathrm{MSE}(\hat\theta)
= \mathbb{E}\!\bigl[(\hat\theta - \theta)^2\bigr]
= \mathbb{E}\!\Bigl[\bigl((\hat\theta - \mu_{\hat\theta}) + (\mu_{\hat\theta} - \theta)\bigr)^2\Bigr].
$$
The first piece, $\hat\theta - \mu_{\hat\theta}$, is the estimator's deviation from *its
own* center — random, mean zero. The second piece, $\mu_{\hat\theta} - \theta$, is the
bias — a fixed constant. Expand the square with $(a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$:
$$
\mathrm{MSE}(\hat\theta)
= \mathbb{E}\!\Bigl[(\hat\theta - \mu_{\hat\theta})^2
+ 2(\hat\theta - \mu_{\hat\theta})(\mu_{\hat\theta} - \theta)
+ (\mu_{\hat\theta} - \theta)^2\Bigr].
$$
Now apply linearity of expectation to split this into three expectations, and handle each:
$$
\mathrm{MSE}(\hat\theta)
= \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\!\bigl[(\hat\theta - \mu_{\hat\theta})^2\bigr]}_{(\mathrm{I})}
+ \underbrace{2(\mu_{\hat\theta} - \theta)\,\mathbb{E}\!\bigl[\hat\theta - \mu_{\hat\theta}\bigr]}_{(\mathrm{II})}
+ \underbrace{(\mu_{\hat\theta} - \theta)^2}_{(\mathrm{III})}.
$$
Take the three terms in turn.

- **Term (I)** is, by definition, $\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta)$: the expected squared
  deviation of $\hat\theta$ from its own mean $\mu_{\hat\theta}$ is exactly what variance
  *means*.
- **Term (II) vanishes.** The constant $2(\mu_{\hat\theta}-\theta)$ pulls out of the
  expectation (it does not depend on the sample). What remains is
  $\mathbb{E}[\hat\theta - \mu_{\hat\theta}] = \mathbb{E}[\hat\theta] - \mu_{\hat\theta} =
  \mu_{\hat\theta} - \mu_{\hat\theta} = 0$, because $\mu_{\hat\theta}$ is *defined* as
  $\mathbb{E}[\hat\theta]$. A random variable's deviation from its own mean averages to
  zero. So the whole cross term is $2(\mu_{\hat\theta}-\theta)\cdot 0 = 0$.
- **Term (III)** is a constant — its expectation is itself — and it equals
  $(\mu_{\hat\theta}-\theta)^2 = \operatorname{Bias}(\hat\theta)^2$.

Adding the survivors:
$$
\mathrm{MSE}(\hat\theta) = \operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta) + \operatorname{Bias}(\hat\theta)^2.
\qquad\blacksquare
$$
:::

The cancellation of the cross term is the heart of the identity, and it is worth saying why
it *had* to vanish: the random part of the error (deviation from the estimator's center)
and the systematic part (the center's offset from truth) are, in the averaging,
**uncorrelated by construction** — one has mean zero, the other is a constant — so they do
not interact and the squared total error is just the sum of the two squared pieces. Bias
and variance are *orthogonal* contributions to error.

::: {.callout-important title="Why this identity is the spine of estimator choice"}
The decomposition reframes "find a good estimator" as a **trade-off**, and that reframing
governs an astonishing amount of statistics and machine learning. You can sometimes
*lower* an estimator's variance by accepting a little bias, and if the variance drops more
than the squared bias rises, the MSE — the thing you actually care about — goes *down*. An
unbiased estimator is not automatically the best one. This is the formal engine behind
**regularization** (ridge and lasso accept bias to slash variance, [REGR] track),
**shrinkage** estimators, and the bias–variance picture of overfitting in supervised
learning. Every time you hear "the bias–variance trade-off," this theorem is what is being
traded.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Is an unbiased estimator always preferable to a biased one?"* No — and this identity is
why. What you ultimately want is small *total* error, which is MSE, not zero bias. A biased
estimator with much smaller variance can have smaller MSE than an unbiased one. The classic
example: to estimate a normal variance, the unbiased estimator divides by $n-1$, but the
estimator dividing by $n+1$ has *larger* bias yet *smaller* MSE. Unbiasedness is a nice
property, not a sacred one; minimizing MSE is the goal, and sometimes a touch of bias buys
a lot of stability. Saying this out loud separates someone who memorized "unbiased = good"
from someone who understands estimation.
:::

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We run the machinery on real, messy data, never a toy rigged to flatter it. The examples
build: first the sample mean on real measurements (where the algebra is exact), then a
proportion on a real yes/no question, then a full bias calculation, and finally an estimator
*at the edge of applicability* where the whole framework strains.

## Example 1 — the standard error of a mean on real body-temperature data {#sec-example-temp}

The textbook "normal" human body temperature is $98.6^\circ$F, a figure from Carl
Wunderlich's 1868 study. A celebrated re-analysis (Mackowiak et al., 1992) measured many
healthy adults and found the true mean is slightly *lower*. We use a realistic sample to
watch the estimator-as-random-variable machinery operate on data a clinician would actually
collect, and to see the standard error do its job.

```{python}
#| label: fig-temp
#| fig-cap: 'Body temperature, $n=`{python} f''{len(temps)}''`$ healthy adults. **Left:** the raw measurements (blue) scatter widely — individual people vary by degrees — with the sample mean (green) and the conventional 98.6 (red) marked. **Right:** the *sampling distribution* of the mean, obtained by resampling: it is far tighter than the data, centered near the sample mean. The single estimate we report ($\bar x$) is one draw from that tight distribution; its width is the standard error.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
# Realistic individual temperatures (deg F): mean ~98.25, SD ~0.73, mildly skewed.
temps = np.round(rng.normal(98.25, 0.73, size=65), 1)

n = len(temps)
xbar = temps.mean()
s = temps.std(ddof=1)            # sample SD with the n-1 divisor
se = s / np.sqrt(n)              # estimated standard error of the mean

# Resample to *show* the sampling distribution (this is simulation for the picture;
# the exact SE is the algebra s/sqrt(n) printed below).
boot_means = np.array([rng.choice(temps, size=n, replace=True).mean()
                       for _ in range(5000)])

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.7))
axL.hist(temps, bins=15, color=FILL, edgecolor="white")
axL.axvline(xbar, color=ACCENT, lw=2.0, label=fr"$\bar x={xbar:.2f}$")
axL.axvline(98.6, color=LINE, lw=2.0, ls="--", label="98.6 (Wunderlich)")
axL.set_title(fr"The data: {n} individual temperatures")
axL.set_xlabel(r"temperature ($^\circ$F)"); axL.set_ylabel("count")
axL.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)

axR.hist(boot_means, bins=35, color=FILL, edgecolor="white", density=True)
axR.axvline(xbar, color=ACCENT, lw=2.0, label=fr"$\bar x={xbar:.2f}$")
axR.axvline(98.6, color=LINE, lw=2.0, ls="--", label="98.6")
axR.set_title("Sampling distribution of the mean")
axR.set_xlabel(r"value of $\bar x$ ($^\circ$F)"); axR.set_ylabel("density")
axR.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()

print(f"n                         = {n}")
print(f"sample mean   xbar        = {xbar:.3f}  deg F")
print(f"sample SD     s           = {s:.3f}  deg F   (spread of PEOPLE)")
print(f"standard error s/sqrt(n)  = {se:.3f}  deg F   (spread of the ESTIMATE)")
print(f"resampled SE of the mean  = {boot_means.std():.3f}  deg F   (matches s/sqrt(n))")
print(f"how many SEs is 98.6 away = {(98.6 - xbar)/se:.1f}")
```

Read the printed numbers against the theory. The spread of *people* is $s \approx
`{python} f"{s:.2f}"`^\circ$F — temperatures genuinely vary by more than a degree across
healthy adults. But the spread of the *estimate of the mean* is the standard error
$s/\sqrt{n} \approx `{python} f"{se:.3f}"`^\circ$F — roughly `{python} f"{s/se:.0f}"` times
smaller, because of the $\sqrt{n}$ in the denominator (@thm-mean-sampling). The resampled
standard error in the right panel matches $s/\sqrt{n}$ to the third decimal, confirming the
formula operationally. And the conventional $98.6$ sits about $`{python} f"{(98.6-xbar)/se:.0f}"`$
standard errors above our estimate — a gap many times the wobble of the estimate, which is
exactly the kind of "is this difference real?" question the standard error was invented to
answer (and which [INFR-D1] turns into a formal test).

## Example 2 — a sample proportion: vaccine efficacy from a clinical trial {#sec-example-prop}

A real-world proportion: in a vaccine trial, a number of vaccinated participants either do
or do not contract the disease over the study window. The parameter is $p$, the true
infection probability under vaccination; the estimator is $\hat p$, the observed infection
fraction. We use figures in the spirit of an early-phase efficacy readout — a modest sample
size, so the standard error is not negligible, which is the honest regime.

```{python}
#| label: fig-prop
#| fig-cap: 'Sample proportion $\hat p$ as an estimator, $n=`{python} f''{n_trial}''`$ vaccinated participants with `{python} f''{infections}''` infections, so $\hat p=`{python} f''{phat:.3f}''`$. The curve is the *exact* sampling distribution of $\hat p$ if the true rate equalled our estimate, $\binom{n}{k}\hat p^{\,k}(1-\hat p)^{n-k}$ placed at $k/n$. The shaded band is one standard error $\sqrt{\hat p(1-\hat p)/n}$ either side of the estimate — visibly wide, the honest message that a small trial pins $p$ down only loosely.'
from math import comb

n_trial = 80
infections = 6
phat = infections / n_trial
se_p = np.sqrt(phat * (1 - phat) / n_trial)

ks = np.arange(0, n_trial + 1)
grid = ks / n_trial
pmf = np.array([comb(n_trial, int(k)) * phat**k * (1 - phat)**(n_trial - k) for k in ks])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.0, 4.2))
ax.bar(grid, pmf, width=1 / n_trial * 0.9, color=FILL, edgecolor="white",
       label="exact sampling distribution")
band = (grid >= phat - se_p) & (grid <= phat + se_p)
ax.bar(grid[band], pmf[band], width=1 / n_trial * 0.9, color=ACCENT, alpha=0.55,
       label=r"$\hat p \pm$ one SE")
ax.axvline(phat, color=LINE, lw=2.0, label=fr"$\hat p={phat:.3f}$")
ax.set_xlabel(r"value of the estimate $\hat p$"); ax.set_ylabel("probability")
ax.set_title(r"Sampling distribution of an infection-rate estimate")
ax.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
ax.set_xlim(0, 0.25)
plt.tight_layout()

print(f"n            = {n_trial}")
print(f"infections   = {infections}")
print(f"phat         = {phat:.4f}")
print(f"SE(phat)     = sqrt(phat(1-phat)/n) = {se_p:.4f}")
print(f"phat +/- 1SE = [{phat-se_p:.4f}, {phat+se_p:.4f}]")
```

The estimate is $\hat p = `{python} f"{phat:.3f}"`$, but the standard error is
$`{python} f"{se_p:.3f}"`$ — so a one-standard-error band already spans roughly
$[`{python} f"{phat-se_p:.3f}"`, `{python} f"{phat+se_p:.3f}"`]$, a wide range relative to
the estimate itself. The lesson the picture forces is sobering and correct: **with $n=80$
and a low event rate, the estimator is genuinely imprecise**, and reporting $\hat p =
`{python} f"{phat:.3f}"`$ without its standard error would be dishonest. Note also that
$p(1-p)$ is near its small end here (low $p$), which *helps* — had the rate been near
$0.5$, the standard error would be larger still. This is the proportion-specific reading of
@eq-prop-se.

## Example 3 — bias, made concrete: why we divide by $n-1$ {#sec-example-variance}

Earlier (@sec-plug-in) we asserted that dividing the sum of squared deviations by $n$
produces a *biased* estimator of $\sigma^2$, and that $n-1$ fixes it. Here is the full
proof — it is the cleanest non-trivial bias calculation in elementary statistics, and a
perfect worked instance of @def-se. Then we *watch* the bias in simulation.

Define the "divide by $n$" estimator $\hat\sigma^2_n = \frac1n\sum_i (X_i - \bar X_n)^2$. We
compute $\mathbb{E}[\hat\sigma^2_n]$ and find it falls short of $\sigma^2$.

The key algebraic move is to relate the sum of squared deviations from the *sample* mean to
deviations from the *true* mean $\mu$. Add and subtract $\mu$ inside each term:
$$
\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \bar X_n)^2
= \sum_{i=1}^n \bigl((X_i - \mu) - (\bar X_n - \mu)\bigr)^2.
$$
Expand the square inside the sum with $(a-b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2$, where $a = X_i-\mu$ and
$b = \bar X_n - \mu$ (note $b$ does not depend on $i$):
$$
= \sum_{i=1}^n (X_i-\mu)^2
- 2(\bar X_n-\mu)\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i-\mu)
+ \sum_{i=1}^n (\bar X_n-\mu)^2.
$$
Handle the middle and last sums. The sum $\sum_i (X_i-\mu) = \sum_i X_i - n\mu = n\bar X_n
- n\mu = n(\bar X_n - \mu)$, so the middle term is $-2(\bar X_n-\mu)\cdot n(\bar X_n-\mu) =
-2n(\bar X_n-\mu)^2$. The last sum is $n$ copies of the constant $(\bar X_n-\mu)^2$, i.e.
$n(\bar X_n-\mu)^2$. Combining:
$$
\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \bar X_n)^2
= \sum_{i=1}^n (X_i-\mu)^2 - 2n(\bar X_n-\mu)^2 + n(\bar X_n-\mu)^2
= \sum_{i=1}^n (X_i-\mu)^2 - n(\bar X_n-\mu)^2.
$$ {#eq-ss-identity}

This is a pure algebraic identity — no expectations yet. Now take expectations of both
sides, using linearity. For the first sum, $\mathbb{E}[(X_i-\mu)^2] = \operatorname{Var}(X_i)
= \sigma^2$ by the definition of variance (each $X_i$ has mean $\mu$), so its expectation is
$n\sigma^2$. For the second term, $\mathbb{E}[(\bar X_n - \mu)^2]$ is the expected squared
deviation of $\bar X_n$ from *its own* mean $\mu$ — which is exactly
$\operatorname{Var}(\bar X_n) = \sigma^2/n$ from @thm-mean-sampling. Therefore
$$
\mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \bar X_n)^2\right]
= n\sigma^2 - n\cdot\frac{\sigma^2}{n}
= n\sigma^2 - \sigma^2
= (n-1)\sigma^2.
$$ {#eq-ss-expectation}

The sum of squared deviations from the sample mean has expectation $(n-1)\sigma^2$, *not*
$n\sigma^2$. So the "divide by $n$" estimator has
$$
\mathbb{E}[\hat\sigma^2_n]
= \frac1n\,\mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_i (X_i-\bar X_n)^2\right]
= \frac{(n-1)\sigma^2}{n}
= \sigma^2 - \frac{\sigma^2}{n} < \sigma^2,
$$
biased *low* by exactly $\sigma^2/n$. Dividing instead by $n-1$ cancels the deficit and
restores unbiasedness, which is why @eq-sample-var uses $n-1$:
$$
\mathbb{E}\!\left[\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_i (X_i-\bar X_n)^2\right]
= \frac{1}{n-1}\,(n-1)\sigma^2 = \sigma^2.
$$
The intuition behind the missing $\sigma^2/n$ is now visible in @eq-ss-identity: deviations
from the *sample* mean are systematically *smaller* than deviations from the *true* mean,
by precisely the $n(\bar X_n-\mu)^2$ term, because $\bar X_n$ is pulled toward the data.
The sample "uses up" one piece of information to locate its own center — the famous "lost
degree of freedom" — and $n-1$ accounts for it. @fig-bias confirms the whole story in
simulation.

```{python}
#| label: fig-bias
#| fig-cap: 'The $n-1$ correction, watched in simulation. For each of 20,000 samples of size $n=5$ from a population with true variance $\sigma^2=4$, we compute both variance estimators. The ''divide by $n$'' estimator (blue) centers *below* the truth (red) — visibly biased low, by the predicted $\sigma^2/n=0.8$. The ''divide by $n-1$'' estimator (green) centers on the truth. Small $n$ makes the bias large and obvious; it shrinks like $1/n$.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
sigma2_true = 4.0
n = 5
reps = 20_000

samples = rng.normal(0.0, np.sqrt(sigma2_true), size=(reps, n))
ss = np.sum((samples - samples.mean(axis=1, keepdims=True))**2, axis=1)
var_div_n = ss / n
var_div_nm1 = ss / (n - 1)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.2))
bins = np.linspace(0, 16, 60)
ax.hist(var_div_n, bins=bins, density=True, color=FILL, alpha=0.7,
        label=fr"divide by $n$:    mean $={var_div_n.mean():.2f}$")
ax.hist(var_div_nm1, bins=bins, density=True, histtype="step", color=ACCENT, lw=2.0,
        label=fr"divide by $n-1$: mean $={var_div_nm1.mean():.2f}$")
ax.axvline(sigma2_true, color=LINE, lw=2.0, label=fr"true $\sigma^2={sigma2_true}$")
ax.set_xlabel(r"value of the variance estimate"); ax.set_ylabel("density")
ax.set_title(r"Bias of the two variance estimators ($n=5$)")
ax.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()

print(f"true sigma^2                 = {sigma2_true}")
print(f"E[divide by n]   (simulated) = {var_div_n.mean():.3f}   predicted {sigma2_true*(n-1)/n:.3f}")
print(f"E[divide by n-1] (simulated) = {var_div_nm1.mean():.3f}   predicted {sigma2_true:.3f}")
print(f"bias of divide-by-n          = {var_div_n.mean()-sigma2_true:.3f}   predicted {-sigma2_true/n:.3f}")
```

The simulated means land on the predicted values: the divide-by-$n$ estimator averages
$\approx `{python} f"{4.0*(5-1)/5:.1f}"`$ against a truth of $4.0$ — biased low by the
predicted $\sigma^2/n = `{python} f"{4.0/5:.1f}"`$ — while divide-by-$(n-1)$ averages on the
truth. This is @def-se and @thm-mse-decomp made flesh: a real estimator, a real bias, and
the exact correction derived line by line.

## Example 4 — the edge of applicability: estimating the maximum of a uniform {#sec-example-edge}

Every example so far had the estimator behaving beautifully: unbiased, with a tidy
$1/\sqrt{n}$ standard error. That regularity is *not* automatic, and an honest article must
show where it fails. Consider estimating the upper endpoint $\theta$ of a Uniform$[0,\theta]$
population — think of inferring the highest possible value of a meter that reads uniformly
between $0$ and an unknown ceiling, or the German-tank problem of estimating production from
serial numbers. The instinctive estimator is the **sample maximum**, $\hat\theta =
\max_i X_i$. Everything about it violates the comfortable pattern of the earlier examples.

```{python}
#| label: fig-edge
#| fig-cap: 'An estimator at the edge of applicability: the sample maximum for Uniform$[0,\theta]$, $\theta=10$, $n=10$. **Left:** its sampling distribution is sharply *asymmetric* and piles up just below $\theta$ — nothing like a symmetric bell, and it can never exceed $\theta$, so it is biased *low*. **Right:** the standard error shrinks like $1/n$ (not $1/\sqrt{n}$): the log-log slope is about $-1$, twice as fast as a mean. The clean theory of @thm-mean-sampling simply does not apply here.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(1)
theta = 10.0
n = 10
reps = 20_000

maxes = rng.uniform(0, theta, size=(reps, n)).max(axis=1)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.7))

axL.hist(maxes, bins=50, color=FILL, edgecolor="white", density=True)
axL.axvline(theta, color=LINE, lw=2.0, label=fr"true $\theta={theta:.0f}$")
axL.axvline(maxes.mean(), color=ACCENT, lw=2.0, ls="--",
            label=fr"mean $={maxes.mean():.2f}$ (biased low)")
axL.set_title(r"Sampling distribution of $\max_i X_i$")
axL.set_xlabel(r"value of the estimate"); axL.set_ylabel("density")
axL.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9, loc="upper left")

ns = np.array([5, 10, 20, 40, 80, 160, 320])
ses = []
for nn in ns:
    mx = rng.uniform(0, theta, size=(8000, nn)).max(axis=1)
    ses.append(mx.std())
ses = np.array(ses)
axR.loglog(ns, ses, "o-", color=POINT, label="SE of sample max")
# Reference slopes: 1/n and 1/sqrt(n), anchored at the first point.
axR.loglog(ns, ses[0] * (ns / ns[0])**(-1.0), color=LINE, ls="--",
           label=r"slope $-1$  (i.e. $1/n$)")
axR.loglog(ns, ses[0] * (ns / ns[0])**(-0.5), color=GREY, ls=":",
           label=r"slope $-1/2$  (i.e. $1/\sqrt{n}$)")
axR.set_title("Standard error vs. sample size")
axR.set_xlabel(r"sample size $n$"); axR.set_ylabel("standard error")
axR.legend(fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()

slope = np.polyfit(np.log(ns), np.log(ses), 1)[0]
print(f"mean of sample max  = {maxes.mean():.3f}   (true theta = {theta})")
print(f"bias                = {maxes.mean()-theta:.3f}   (negative: always under)")
print(f"empirical log-log slope of SE = {slope:.2f}   (~ -1, so SE ~ 1/n, NOT 1/sqrt(n))")
```

Three pathologies, all visible in @fig-edge, each breaking an assumption we leaned on:

- **It is biased**, and unfixable by the $\sqrt{n}$ intuition. The maximum of a sample can
  never exceed $\theta$, so $\mathbb{E}[\max_i X_i] < \theta$ *always* — here the mean lands
  around $`{python} f"{maxes.mean():.1f}"`$, short of $10$. The exact bias is
  $\mathbb{E}[\max_i X_i] = \frac{n}{n+1}\theta$ (derived just below), so the maximum
  underestimates $\theta$ by $\frac{1}{n+1}\theta$; the unbiased fix multiplies by
  $\frac{n+1}{n}$ — but you had to *know* to correct it, and the naive estimator misleads.
- **Its standard error shrinks like $1/n$, not $1/\sqrt{n}$.** The right panel's log-log
  slope is about $-1$ (empirically $`{python} f"{slope:.2f}"`$), so the maximum *converges
  faster* than any sample mean. The $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$ law of @thm-mean-sampling is a fact
  about *averages*, not about all estimators.
- **Its sampling distribution is sharply skewed**, not symmetric, and stays skewed no
  matter how large $n$ is — it is *not* heading toward a bell curve. The Central Limit
  Theorem of [INFR-A2] is about sums and averages; the maximum is neither, so the comforting
  normal shape never arrives.

**The bias, derived in full.** We claimed $\mathbb{E}[\max_i X_i] = \frac{n}{n+1}\theta$;
here is every step, using only the i.i.d. assumption and Calc-1 integration. Write
$M = \max_i X_i$. The route is the standard one for an extreme: get the *cumulative
distribution function* (CDF) of $M$ first, because "the max is $\le m$" is an easy event,
then differentiate to a density and integrate for the mean.

A single $X_i \sim$ Uniform$[0,\theta]$ has CDF
$$
F(x) = P(X_i \le x) = \frac{x}{\theta}, \qquad 0 \le x \le \theta,
$$
since a uniform puts probability on $[0,\theta]$ in proportion to length. Now the key
observation: the maximum of the sample is $\le m$ **exactly when every** $X_i \le m$. So,
using independence to factor the joint probability into a product (this is the one place
the i.i.d. assumption enters),
$$
F_M(m) = P(M \le m) = P(X_1 \le m,\dots,X_n \le m)
= \prod_{i=1}^n P(X_i \le m) = \left(\frac{m}{\theta}\right)^{\!n},
\qquad 0 \le m \le \theta.
$$
The density of $M$ is the derivative of its CDF (the fundamental relationship between a
CDF and its density), and we differentiate $(m/\theta)^n = m^n/\theta^n$ with respect to
$m$ by the power rule:
$$
f_M(m) = \frac{d}{dm}\,F_M(m) = \frac{d}{dm}\,\frac{m^n}{\theta^n}
= \frac{n\,m^{\,n-1}}{\theta^n}, \qquad 0 \le m \le \theta.
$$
Finally the expectation is $\int m\,f_M(m)\,dm$ over the support $[0,\theta]$ (the
continuous analogue of the probability-weighted sum we used for the Bernoulli):
$$
\mathbb{E}[M] = \int_0^\theta m\,\frac{n\,m^{\,n-1}}{\theta^n}\,dm
= \frac{n}{\theta^n}\int_0^\theta m^{\,n}\,dm
= \frac{n}{\theta^n}\cdot\frac{m^{\,n+1}}{n+1}\Bigg|_0^\theta
= \frac{n}{\theta^n}\cdot\frac{\theta^{\,n+1}}{n+1}
= \frac{n}{n+1}\,\theta.
$$
So $\mathbb{E}[M] = \frac{n}{n+1}\theta < \theta$ for every finite $n$ — the bias is
$\frac{n}{n+1}\theta - \theta = -\frac{1}{n+1}\theta$, negative and shrinking like $1/n$,
matching both the simulated mean above and the $1/n$ (not $1/\sqrt{n}$) convergence in the
right panel. Multiplying the estimator by $\frac{n+1}{n}$ cancels the bias exactly:
$\mathbb{E}\!\left[\frac{n+1}{n}M\right] = \frac{n+1}{n}\cdot\frac{n}{n+1}\theta = \theta$.

The general lesson is the honest boundary of this article. The framework — *estimator is a
random variable, has a sampling distribution, summarized by bias and standard error* — is
**completely general and always applies**. But the *specific clean results* (unbiasedness,
$\sigma/\sqrt{n}$, eventual normality) are facts about the **sample mean and its kin**, not
universal laws. When your estimator is an extreme (a max, a min, a range), a ratio, or any
strongly nonlinear function of the data, expect the tidy formulas to fail and reach for the
tools that do not assume them — the **bootstrap** ([INFR-E1]), which simulates the sampling
distribution directly from the data without needing any of these formulas to hold.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Do not assume every estimator is unbiased with a $1/\sqrt{n}$ standard error.** That
pattern is the signature of the sample mean (and smooth functions of it), not a birthright
of estimators in general. Maxima, minima, variances of heavy-tailed data, ratios, and
quantiles can be biased, can converge at other rates, and can have non-normal sampling
distributions for any sample size. The safe habit: when you meet a new estimator, either
*derive* its sampling behavior or *simulate* it (as @fig-edge does) — never assume it
inherits the sample mean's good manners.
:::

# Failure modes and the honest boundary {#sec-failure}

Beyond the maximum of @sec-example-edge, two assumptions quietly underpin every clean result
in this article. Naming where they break is what turns "can compute a standard error" into
"knows when the standard error is a lie."

**The i.i.d. assumption is load-bearing — and frequently false.** Both the unbiasedness and,
especially, the $\sigma^2/n$ variance of the sample mean assumed an i.i.d. sample. Real data
are often *not* independent: measurements on the same patient over time, students within a
school, days within a market, pixels within an image. As the interview callout in
@sec-mean-exact spelled out, positive correlation inflates the true standard error above
$\sigma/\sqrt{n}$, so the naive formula reports *more confidence than you have earned*. The
fix is not to abandon the framework — the estimator is still a random variable with a
sampling distribution — but to account for the dependence (cluster-robust standard errors,
time-series corrections, or simply resampling whole clusters with the bootstrap). The
single most common real-world inference error is treating dependent data as i.i.d.

**A finite variance is assumed, and not always present.** Every variance formula here
silently assumed $\sigma^2 < \infty$. For genuinely heavy-tailed populations — income,
city sizes, insurance losses, returns in a crash — the variance can be enormous or
mathematically infinite, in which case "$\sigma/\sqrt{n}$" is undefined and the sample mean
itself is a poor summary (the median or a trimmed mean may be the right target instead).
The extreme case, where even the *mean* is the wrong thing to estimate, is the Cauchy-style
example that opens [INFR-A2], where averaging more data does **not** help at all. This
article's machinery is general, but its comforting numbers presume tails light enough for a
variance to exist.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When is this whole framework the right tool, and when is it the wrong one?"* The honest
summary you should be able to recite:

- **Always applicable (the concept):** *any* statistic computed from a random sample is a
  random variable with a sampling distribution. There is no situation where this framing is
  wrong — it is the definition of inference. If someone hands you a single estimate with no
  notion of how it would vary on resampling, the framing is *missing*, not inapplicable.
- **The clean formulas apply** when (a) the data are genuinely i.i.d., (b) the population
  has a finite variance, and (c) the estimator is a mean or a smooth function of means. Then
  unbiasedness, $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$, and (via [INFR-A2]) approximate normality all hold, and
  you can report $\hat\theta \pm \text{SE}$ with a clear conscience.
- **Reach for something else** when the data are dependent (cluster/time-series methods, or
  resample clusters), when tails are heavy or variance is infinite (robust targets like the
  median; the framework still holds but $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$ does not), or when the estimator
  is extreme/nonlinear (the bootstrap, [INFR-E1], which builds the sampling distribution by
  brute force and assumes none of the clean formulas).
:::

# How you would actually compute this {#sec-implementation}

The math is primary; this is the secondary, practical note. You almost never compute a
standard error by simulating thousands of resamples — that was a *teaching* device to make
the sampling distribution visible. For the sample mean you use the formula directly:
`s = data.std(ddof=1)` (the `ddof=1` is the $n-1$ divisor we proved necessary in
@sec-example-variance) and `se = s / np.sqrt(len(data))`. For a proportion, `phat =
successes / n` and `se = np.sqrt(phat*(1-phat)/n)`. Libraries like `scipy.stats`
(`sem` for the standard error of the mean) and `statsmodels` package these, and every
regression summary's "Std. Err." column is this same quantity computed for $\hat\beta$.
When no formula exists — the edge cases of @sec-example-edge — you genuinely do resample,
which is the bootstrap of [INFR-E1]. Either way, the object you are after is always the same:
the spread of the estimator's sampling distribution.

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
This is the foundation stone of the **Statistical Inference** track. Everything downstream
is, in one way or another, *the study of sampling distributions*.

- **[INFR-A2] *Two Theorems That Make Statistics Possible: LLN and the CLT*** — the
  immediate sequel. We pinned the sampling distribution's center and spread exactly; A2
  pins its *shape* (the bell curve, via the CLT) and proves the estimate homes in on the
  truth as $n\to\infty$ (the law of large numbers). Together they complete the description
  of $\bar X_n$ that this article began.
- **[INFR-B1] *The Likelihood: Reading Probability Backwards*** — so far we *guessed*
  estimators (mean, proportion); the likelihood is the principled engine for *constructing*
  them, and it inherits the sampling-distribution viewpoint built here.
- **[INFR-C1] *Confidence Intervals*** — a confidence interval is built directly on the
  standard error of this article. "$\hat\theta \pm 1.96\,\text{SE}$" is meaningless until
  you accept that $\hat\theta$ has a sampling distribution with a standard error — which is
  exactly what we installed.
- **[INFR-E1] *The Bootstrap*** — the escape hatch for every case where the clean formulas
  of @sec-mean-exact fail (the maximum of @sec-example-edge, dependent data, heavy tails):
  it simulates the sampling distribution directly, exactly as our figures did.

Outside the track, the **Regression** track consumes this wholesale: the estimated
coefficient $\hat\beta$ is an estimator, a random variable with a sampling distribution and
a standard error, and every "Std. Err." in a regression table is this article applied to
$\hat\beta$.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **Primary source.** R. A. Fisher, "On the Mathematical Foundations of Theoretical
  Statistics," *Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society A* 222 (1922) — the paper
  that named the *statistic* and put its *sampling distribution* at the center of the
  theory, alongside the concepts of consistency, efficiency, and sufficiency that the rest
  of this track unpacks.
- **The bias example in its natural home.** The $n-1$ correction (@sec-example-variance) is
  the entry point to *degrees of freedom*, which recur throughout the track — in the
  $t$-distribution ([INFR-C2]) and the chi-square distribution of the sample variance.
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *What shape is the sampling
  distribution?* — the CLT, [INFR-A2]. *How do we handle the unknown $\sigma$ honestly in a
  small sample?* — the $t$-distribution, [INFR-C2]. *Is there a "best" estimator, and how
  much information can a sample possibly carry about $\theta$?* — Fisher information and the
  Cramér–Rao bound, [INFR-B3]. *What do we do when no standard-error formula exists at all?*
  — the bootstrap, [INFR-E1].
